# Notifications Configuration Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This topic provides details on the elements to configure for notifications. For more information on
notifications, see [Mule Server Notifications](mule-server-notifications).

## Notifications

Registers listeners for notifications, and associates interfaces with specific events.

### Attributes of Notifications

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dynamic</code></p></td><td><p>If the notification manager is dynamic, you can set up the listeners to be registered dynamically at runtime. Otherwise, some parts of Mule will cache the notification configurations for efficiency reasons and will not generate events for newly-enabled notifications or listeners.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>boolean</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: <code>false</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Child Elements of Notifications

Use the `notification` and `disable-notification` elements to configure the relationship between
interfaces and events.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Cardinality</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>notification</code></p></td><td><p>0..*</p></td><td><p>Associates an event with an interface. Listeners that implement the interface receive instances of the event.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>disable-notification</code></p></td><td><p>0..*</p></td><td><p>Blocks the association of an event with a particular interface. This filters events after the association is made with the interface, so it takes precedence over other elements, such as <code>notification-listener</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>notification-listener</code></p></td><td><p>0..*</p></td><td><p>Registers a bean as a listener with the notification system. Events are dispatched by reflection, which means that the listener receives all events associated with the interfaces it implements.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Notification

Associates an event with an interface. Listeners that implement the interface will receive instances
of the event.

### Attributes of Notification

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>event-class</code></p></td><td><p>The class name of the notification event to deliver to the interface. You can use this instead of the <code>event</code> attribute to specify a custom class.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>class name</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>event</code></p></td><td><p>The notification event to deliver.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>notificationTypes</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>interface-class</code></p></td><td><p>The class name of the interface that receives the notification event.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>class name</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>interface</code></p></td><td><p>The name of the interface that receives the notification event.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>notificationTypes</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

There are no child elements for `notification`.

## Disable Notification

Blocks the association of an event with a particular interface. This filters events after the
association is made with the interface, so it takes precedence over other elements, such as
`notification-listener`.

### Attributes of disable-notification

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>event-class</code></p></td><td><p>The class name of the event that is no longer delivered to an interface. You can use this instead of the <code>event</code> attribute to specify a custom class.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>class name</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>event</code></p></td><td><p>The event you no longer want to deliver.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>notificationTypes</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>interface-class</code></p></td><td><p>The class name of the interface that no longer receives the event.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>class name</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>interface</code></p></td><td><p>The name of the interface that no longer receives the event.<br><strong>Type</strong>: <code>notificationTypes</code><br><strong>Required</strong>: no<br><strong>Default</strong>: none</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

There are no child elements for `disable-notification`.

## Notification Listener

Registers a bean as a listener with the notification system. Events are dispatched by reflection,
which means that the listener receives all events associated with the interfaces it implements.

## Notification Types

You can specify the following types of notifications using the `event` attribute of the
`notification` and `disable-notification` elements:

- ASYNC-MESSAGE
- CONNECTION
- CONNECTOR-MESSAGE
- CONTEXT
- CUSTOM
- EXCEPTION
- EXCEPTION-STRATEGY
- MANAGEMENT
- MESSAGE-PROCESSOR
- PIPELINE-MESSAGE
- ROUTING
- SECURITY
- TRANSACTION
---

# On-Error Components

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

When an error occurs in a Mule app, an Error Handler component routes the error to the first
On-Error component (On Error Continue or On Error Propagate) configuration that matches the Mule
error, such as `HTTP:NOT_FOUND`, `DB:CONNECTIVITY`, or `ANY` (the default for all Mule errors). If
no error handling is configured for the error, the app follows a
[default error handling](error-handling#default_error_handling) process.

![A palette menu for selecting error handling strategies in Mule applications](_images/mruntime-error-handler.png)

Figure 1. Error Handling Components in Studio

## On-Error Components Behavior

On-Error components differ in the way they affect their _owner_, that is, the Flow or Try scope
where they are defined. Also, On-Error components can be defined inside a Global Error Handler,
affecting the entire Mule application.

### On Error Propagate

Executes but propagates the error to a higher level, such as a containing scope (for example, to a
Flow that contains a Try scope where the error occurs) or external flow containing a Flow Reference
to the flow in which an error occurs. The error breaks the ownerâs execution and propagates to that
higher level, causing a failure at that level. In addition, any transaction the owner handles is
rolled back. However, note that the transaction is not rolled back if another component (one that
does not own On Error Propagate) created the transaction.

![An error handling configuration using 'On Error Propagate' in a Mule flow](_images/mruntime-on-error-propagate.png)

### On Error Continue

Executes and uses the result of the execution as the result of its owner, as if the owner completed
the execution successfully. Any transaction the owner handles is committed. However, note that the
transaction is not committed if another component (one that does not own On Error Continue) created
the transaction.

## Error Matching

To help you identify and handle potential errors when designing a Mule app, On-Error components list
the errors that the runtime engine and operations configured in the app can throw. You can select
the errors you want to handle.

- Matching based on error type:

  On-Error components can perform matching based on [error types](mule-error-concept#error_types)
  you select. The list of selectable error types depends on the module and connector operations
  within the flow or scope to which the On-Error component applies. It also lists `EXPRESSION` and
  `STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED` errors, which the runtime can throw.

  ![An error type selection menu in a Mule applicationâs error handling configuration](_images/mruntime-on-error-type-selection.png)

- Matching based on a condition (for more advanced use cases):

  You can perform error matching based on a `when` condition that you define in the **When** field
  of an On-Error component. For example, you might map the component to fatal errors, which have
  error messages that contain the word "fatal":

  ```xml
  <on-error-continue
    enableNotifications="true" logException="true"
    type="ANY"
    when='error.cause.message contains "fatal"'/>
  ```

  In the example, every error that matches the `when` expression is handled by `on-error-continue`.
  You can also add a restrictive type such as `type="HTTP:CONNECTIVITY"` so that the error handler
  handles only the errors that match the specified type and condition.

Note that matching conditions are evaluated sequentially, in the order in which the On-Error
components reside in the error handler. For example, if you want to handle `VALIDATION:NOT_NULL` in
one way but handle all other errors a different way, provide an On-Error component configuration for
that error before a component that captures the remaining errors (identified as `ANY` by the second
On-Error component). Remember that default error handling takes place for errors you do not
explicitly match or use ANY to capture.

## Configuration and Use

In most cases, you configure On-Error components within the scope-like Error Handler component.
Though each flow contains only one built-in Error Handler, the Error Handler can contain as many
On-Error components as you require, and each On-Error component can contain any number of other
components, such as Loggers and other components that you configure to handle errors captured by the
On-Error component.

Because an Error Handler component is built into Flow and Try components, you can use On-Error
components for errors that occur within the scope of a flow or within a Try component. For examples,
see [Using On-Error Components to Handle Messaging Errors](error-handling#on_error_continue).

You can also use On-Error components in a separate Error Handler configuration that is referenced
from a Flow component or Try scope (see
[Referencing a Global Error Handler](error-handling#global_error_handler)).

By default, On-Error components also log matching errors and enable error notifications for
occurrences of the errors. You can disable these settings by modifying the component properties in
Studio or by editing the configuration XML. You can also configure notification listeners in your
Mule application so that they can react to the notification events.

### Error Handling within a Flow

Within a flowâs built-in Error Handler component, you can set up one or more On-Error components to
match error types and expressions. The following example outlines the general structure for handling
errors that occur within a given flow. One flow uses On Error Continue (`on-error-continue`) in its
Error Handler component. The other flow uses On Error Propagate (`on-error-propagate`).

```xml
<flow name="catch">
  <!-- flow logic -->
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-continue>
      <!-- error handling logic -->
    </on-error-continue>
  </error-handler>
</flow>

<flow name="rollback">
  <!-- flow logic -->
  <error-handler>
    <on-error-propagate>
      <!-- error handling logic -->
    </on-error-propagate>
  </error-handler>
</flow>
```

For examples, see
[Using On-Error Components to Handle Messaging Errors](error-handling#on_error_continue).

### Reuse of On-Error Scopes

Just as you can share error handlers by exposing them globally and referencing them in flows and try
scopes, you can also reuse On-Error components. You can define and name an On-Error component
globally and then reference from an Error Handler component.

Example: XML Configuration for Referenced On-Error

```xml
<on-error-propagate name="sharedPropagateHandler">
  <logger message="An unhandled error has occurred."/>
</on-error-propagate>

<on-error-continue type="EXPRESSION" name="sharedContinueHandler">
  <logger message="An expression failed to evaluate."/>
</on-error-continue>

<error-handler name="reusingHandlers">
  <on-error ref="sharedContinueHandler"/>
  <on-error-continue type="ROUTING">
    <logger message="An expression failed to evaluate."/>
  </on-error-continue>
  <on-error ref="sharedPropagateHandler"/>
</error-handler>
```

For other reuse examples, see
[Referencing a Global Error Handler](error-handling#global_error_handler).

## Error Handling Example

The following error handlers are configured to produce
`{ "MyError": "value was expected to be null" }` for a `VALIDATION:NOT_NULL` error, rather than
`{ "messageANY" : "Some other error" }`. The string `value was expected to be null` is the
description of the `VALIDATION:NOT_NULL` error. The Is Null component can throw this error. Is Null
belongs to the Validation module.

```xml
<error-handler >
  <on-error-continue
    enableNotifications="true"
    logException="true"
    type="VALIDATION:NOT_NULL">
    <ee:transform >
      <ee:message>
        <ee:set-payload><![CDATA[%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
MyError : error.description as String
}]]></ee:set-payload>
      </ee:message>
    </ee:transform>
  </on-error-continue>
  <on-error-continue
    enableNotifications="true"
    logException="true"
    type="ANY">
    <ee:transform>
      <ee:message >
        <ee:set-payload ><![CDATA[%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
"messageANY" : "Some other error"
}
]]></ee:set-payload>
      </ee:message>
    </ee:transform>
  </on-error-continue>
</error-handler>
```

## On-Error Components Reference

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Type (<code>type</code>)</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Mule error type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A comma-separated list of Mule error types that defines the errors handled by this on-error strategy.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>When (<code>when</code>)</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>DataWeave Expression</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A matching expression that defines the condition to trigger the execution of this on-error strategy. The expression must evaluate to a boolean value (<code>true</code> or <code>false</code>).</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Enable Notifications (<code>enableNotifications</code>)</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies if this strategy fires a notification of type <code>ExceptionNotification</code> when an error occurs. The default value is <code>true</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Log Exception (<code>logException</code>)</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies if the error is logged, at level <code>ERROR</code>, before this strategy handles it. The default value is <code>true</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Mule Errors](mule-error-concept)
- [Try Scope](try-scope-concept)
- [Mule Runtime Engine Notifications](mule-server-notifications)

# OpenTelemetry Support in Mule Runtime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

OpenTelemetry is an observability standard consisting of specifications, APIs, and SDKs. It helps
you instrument, generate, collect, and export telemetry data, such as metrics, logs, and traces, to
analyze software behavior.

OpenTelemetry enables Mule runtime engine to provide observability into the behavior of Mule
applications. With tracing context propagation, Mule also brings observability to interactions
between Mule apps and non-Mule components that leverage this standard.

Mule supports generating and exporting OpenTelemetry traces, logs, and metrics.

## Before You Begin

To use the OpenTelemetry Mule features, you must be familiar with:

- Distributed tracing concepts, including traces and spans.

  If youâre new to distributed tracing and OpenTelemetry, we recommend starting with
  [What is OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/what-is-opentelemetry/) and
  [Understanding distributed tracing](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/concepts/observability-primer/#understanding-distributed-tracing).

- [How to export telemetry data to third-party monitoring systems](../../monitoring/telemetry-exporter).

Before starting with the observability standard, you must have:

- For traces and logs, Mule runtime 4.11.0 or later.
- For metrics, Mule runtime 4.12.0 or later.
- For OpenTelemetry tracing context propagation, Anypoint Connector for HTTP (HTTP Connector) 1.8 or
  later.
- Mule runtime Direct Telemetry Stream.

> [!NOTE] Direct Telemetry Stream is available only for Advanced and Titanium tiers of MuleSoft
> Anypoint Platform.

## Tracing a Mule Application

Mule leverages OpenTelemetry to provide:

- Application execution observability

  Tracing a Mule runtime application allows you to observe its execution while maintaining the
  mental model of a Mule engineer. Its main objective is to clearly show how the Mule application
  processes messages, helping you to develop, monitor, and troubleshoot more effectively.

- Distributed tracing

  Tracing also enables you to track Mule application interactions. When multiple systems and
  services are involved, distributed tracing tracks requests as they flow through distributed
  environments, providing a comprehensive view of the overall execution.

Distributed tracing is achieved via the tracing context propagation. For example, if an HTTP
Listener receives [W3C Trace Context](https://www.w3.org/TR/trace-context/) headers, the flow span
in Mule acquires the clientâs remote parent span, the span where the HTTP call originated. Mule
automatically propagates the current span to the server endpoints when using the HTTP Requester
component.

The span context format supported for receiving and propagating the context is the
[W3C Trace Context](https://www.w3.org/TR/trace-context/) format.

### Mule Span Data

A trace is a sequence of spans representing a complete operation in a distributed system. Spans can
be nested and represent a unit of work or operation. Each span includes:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Categories</th><th>Description</th><th>Example Values</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Name</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the span</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>mule:set-variable</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Parent Span ID</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Span ID of current spanâs parent</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>86838830d494d679</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Start and End Timestamps</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Earliest and latest timestamp for a span</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>2021-10-22 16:04:01.209458162 +0000 UTC</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span Context</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Data that uniquely identifies the span: trace ID, span ID, trace flags, and trace state</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Attributes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Key-value pairs containing metadata used to annotate a span to carry information about the operation itâs tracking.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>{ âlocationâ: âflow/processors/2â, âcorrelation.idâ: â1234abcdâ }</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span Events</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A structured log message or annotation on a span, used to denote a meaningful, unique point in time during the spanâs duration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>{ "name": "", "message": "OK", "timestamp": "2021-10-22 16:04:01.209512872 +0000 UTC" }</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span Links</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Links that associate one span with one or more spans, indicating a causal relationship.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span Status</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Status attached to a span. You can set a span status when a known error, such as an exception, occurs in the app code.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>ERROR</code>, <code>OK</code>, <code>UNSET</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span Kind</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Information about how the trace is assembled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>CLIENT</code>, <code>INTERNAL</code>, <code>PRODUCER</code></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example of Flow Tracing Feature

When tracing a flow execution, each Mule component execution involved in processing the message is
represented as a span. The component spans describe the relevant aspects of the execution. They can
range from a single span for a simple component, such as the Set Payload, to more complex
structures, such as a Batch Job.

Batch Instance

The Mule runtime traces complex components, such as Batch Jobs.

![A batch flow including Listener and Set Payload components, and a more complex structure](_images/otel-batch-instance.png)

Spans Generated

The trace shows how the first step of the Batch Job processes each batch record, with each record
span containing a child span that represents the unit of work the Logger component performs. Next,
it displays the Batch Aggregator span, along with the For Each component and the processors within
it, such as Transform Message and Logger. Then, the trace displays the other two batch steps and
their respective components. Finally, it shows the Batch On Complete span, along with the Logger
component that appears after the Batch Job in the flow.

### Example of Tracing Context Propagation Feature

In this example, one Mule app sends a request to another Mule app:

![A flow including a Listener, Logger, and Request components of an HTTP app](_images/otel-mule-app1.png)

![A flow showing the process flow for an asynchronous application](_images/otel-mule-app2.png)

Distributed tracing captures both flows as part of the same trace because OpenTelemetry is
instrumented in Mule and HTTP Connector.

### Mule Errors in Tracing

If the execution of the unit of work represented by a span produces an error, the spanâs status is
set to `Error`. MuleSoft follows the semantic convention defined by OpenTelemetry that adds one or
more `exception` events to the span:

- `exception.type`: Mule error type
- `exception.message`: Mule error message
- `exception.stacktrace`: Mule flow stack string representation

In this example, when an error is produced, the span corresponding to the Raise Error component has
an `Error` status:

![A flow of a telemetry flow with error handling](_images/otel-errors-in-tracing.png)

Because this flow lacks error handling, the error propagates and fails the flow execution, causing
the `Error` status to propagate to the flow span. A detailed view of the span also shows the
associated exception events.

### HTTP Conventions

The HTTP Listener and HTTP Request operations contribute additional attributes to the spans,
providing more context for tracing the HTTP interactions.

For flows with an HTTP Listener:

- Span name: Combination of HTTP Method and path. For example, `POST /api`.
- Span kind: `SERVER`.

Additional attributes for an HTTP Listener associated flow span:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Attribute</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>net.host.name</code></p></td><td><p>Host address of the HTTP Listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>net.host.port</code></p></td><td><p>Port of the HTTP Listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>http.user_agent</code></p></td><td><p>User agent of the received request</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>http.route</code></p></td><td><p>Path of the HTTP Listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>http.method</code></p></td><td><p>HTTP method</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>http.flavor</code></p></td><td><p>HTTP version</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For flows with an HTTP Request:

- Span name: HTTP Method. For example, `GET`.
- Span kind: `CLIENT`.

Additional attributes for an HTTP Request associated flow span:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Attribute</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>http.url</code></p></td><td><p>Target URL</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>net.host.port</code></p></td><td><p>Port of the HTTP Request</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>net.peer.name</code></p></td><td><p>Target IP address</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>http.route</code></p></td><td><p>Path of the HTTP Request</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>http.flavor</code></p></td><td><p>HTTP version</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## OpenTelemetry Logs

Mule runtime implements its [logging](logging-in-mule) by leveraging Log4j, where you can configure
appenders and logging levels to send logs to different destinations.

The OpenTelemetry logging export is implemented along with Log4j logging. This means you can
configure OpenTelemetry logging export on top of your existing logging setup. This flexible
architecture doesnât force a full transition to OpenTelemetry.

Exported logs include application, domain, and Mule runtime server logs.

### Mule Log Record Data

OpenTelemetry logging is a sequence of log records with a unified data model, where each record
corresponds to a Java log call. Each log record contains:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Categories</th><th>Description</th><th>Example Values</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Timestamp</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time of the Java log call</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>2021-10-22 16:04:01.209458162 +0000 UTC</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Observed Timestamp</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time of the OpenTelemetry log event registration</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>2021-10-22 16:04:01.209458162 +0000 UTC</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Trace ID</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Trace where the log event originated. Not present if the log occurred outside an application trace.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>4a7b9e1d5c3f8a0e2b6d1f7c8a9e0d2c</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span ID</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Span where the log event originated. Not present if the log occurred outside an application trace.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>4b7f3c798460</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Trace Flags</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>W3C trace flags corresponding to the trace where the log event originated. Not present if the log occurred outside an application trace.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>01x</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Severity Text</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Severity of the log event. Mule maps this value to the equivalent Log4j level during export.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>INFO</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Body</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Body of the log record. Also known as log message.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>Stopping flow: test-flow</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Instrumentation Scope</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Fully qualified name of the Java class where the Java log call originated</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>org.mule.runtime.core.internal.lifecycle.AbstractLifecycleManager</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Attributes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Key-value pairs that annotate a log record with context metadata. Includes Log4j MDC entries.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>{ âlocationâ: âflow/processors/2â, âcorrelation.idâ: â1234abcdâ }</code></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### MDC Propagation

Any entry added to the Log4j MDC is added to the OpenTelemetry logs as Log Record attributes, so all
Log4j MDC data is exported as part of OpenTelemetry logs.

For adding custom attributes on both traces and logs, see [Custom Attributes](#custom-attributes).

### Correlation with Traces

- When using OpenTelemetry logs:

  OpenTelemetry Log Records include fields for the trace ID and span ID. When tracing is enabled,
  Mule runtime exports OpenTelemetry logs that include these values, enabling correlation between
  application traces and logs.

- When using Log4j logs:

  If you export traces only, Mule runtime still adds the trace ID and span ID to the
  [Log4j MDC](logging-mdc) under the keys `trace_id` and `span_id`.

## OpenTelemetry Metrics

Metrics provide visibility into the health and operational performance of the Mule runtime, Mule
applications and the Direct Telemetry Stream export.

### Message Processing Metrics

_Instrumentation scope: \`org.mule.runtime.flow.statistics\`_

These metrics track message throughput and processing errors at the flow level. They reflect the
core message and response lifecycle of a Mule application.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Metric</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dispatched.messages</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Total number of messages dispatched by sources for flow processing.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>received.events</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Total events received by flows for processing.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>execution.errors</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Errors encountered during flow processing.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>fatal.errors</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Unrecoverable errors that terminated flow processing.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

These metrics are useful for understanding application throughput and detecting error rate trends
over time.

### Flow Inventory Metrics

_Instrumentation scope: \`org.mule.runtime.flow.statistics\`_

These metrics provide a snapshot of the applicationâs flow topology. They report how many flows of
each category are declared in the application configuration and how many are currently active
(started).

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Metric</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>declared-trigger-flows</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Number of flows with message sources (HTTP listeners, schedulers, etc.)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>active-trigger-flows</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Trigger flows currently in a started state.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>declared-private-flows</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Number of sub-flows and private flows (no message source).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>active-private-flows</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Private flows currently in a started state.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>declared-apikit-flows</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Number of flows generated by APIKit from an API specification.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>active-apikit-flows</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>APIKit flows currently in a started state.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Error Metrics

_Instrumentation scope: \`org.mule.runtime.errors\`_

These metrics break down runtime errors by their Mule error type, providing more granular error
visibility than the aggregate `execution.errors` counter.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Metric</th><th>Type</th><th>Attributes</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>error-count</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p><code>error-type</code>, <code>mule.artifact.id</code></p></td><td><p>Count of errors classified by Mule error type</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The `error-type` attribute contains the Mule error identifier (e.g., `MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING`,
`HTTP:NOT_FOUND`, `MULE:SOURCE_RESPONSE_SEND`), enabling per-type trend analysis and alerting.

> [!NOTE] `error-count` and `execution.errors` are tracked independently. `execution.errors` can
> report a higher total because it counts all execution-level errors, including those not classified
> into a specific error type.

### Runtime Alerts Metrics

_Instrumentation scope: \`org.mule.runtime.diagnostics\`_

These metrics expose the Mule runtime built in alerting system, tracking operational conditions that
may require attention.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Metric</th><th>Type</th><th>Attributes</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>alert-fuseboard</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p><code>mule.alert.name</code>, <code>mule.alert.context</code>, <code>mule.artifact.id</code></p></td><td><p>Number of runtime alerts triggered</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Known alert types:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Alert Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>OTEL_EXPORT_QUEUE_FULL</code></p></td><td><p>The OpenTelemetry export queue reached capacity, causing telemetry data to be delayed or dropped</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>ASYNC_LOGGER_RINGBUFFER_FULL</code></p></td><td><p>The Log4j async logger ring buffer is full, indicating logging backpressure</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The `mule.alert.context` attribute provides additional detail about the alert (e.g.,
`Resource: log records, Action: delayed`).

### JVM Metrics

_Instrumentation scope: \`io.opentelemetry.runtime-telemetry\`_

Standard OpenTelemetry JVM instrumentation metrics providing visibility into the underlying Java
Virtual Machine.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Metric</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>jvm.memory.used</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Current memory usage by pool (heap/non-heap)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.memory.committed</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Memory committed by the JVM per pool</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.memory.limit</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Maximum memory available per pool</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.memory.used_after_last_gc</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Memory used after the most recent GC</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.gc.duration</code></p></td><td><p>Histogram</p></td><td><p>Garbage collection pause durations by GC name</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.thread.count</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Active threads by state</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.class.count</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Currently loaded classes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.class.loaded</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Total classes loaded since JVM start</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.class.unloaded</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Total classes unloaded since JVM start</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.cpu.count</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Available processors</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.cpu.recent_utilization</code></p></td><td><p>Gauge</p></td><td><p>Recent CPU utilization (0.0 to 1.0)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>jvm.cpu.time</code></p></td><td><p>Counter</p></td><td><p>Cumulative CPU time consumed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Batch Processing

## Direct Telemetry Stream from Mule Runtime

> [!NOTE] This feature is available only for Advanced and Titanium tiers of MuleSoft Anypoint
> Platform.

Starting with Mule runtime 4.11.0, you can export OpenTelemetry traces and logs outside Anypoint
Monitoring. This allows Mule runtime to export its OpenTelemetry data to a customer-managed
OpenTelemetry collector instead of the Anypoint Monitoring OpenTelemetry pipeline.

This solution uses a shared responsibility model. Size your environment to support telemetry export.
Sizing depends on factors such as Mule app and third-party observability platform performance. Test
to discover additional factors.

When using this direct telemetry stream feature, Anypoint Monitoring OpenTelemetry features, such as
Telemetry Exporter, arenât available.

### Recommended Architecture for Direct Telemetry Stream

Mule runtime supports exporting all its telemetry data using the OpenTelemetry protocol (OTLP) and
binary/protobuf format. You can configure an endpoint for each signal type (traces, logs, and
metrics), and data is sent to that endpoint. However, we strongly recommend using a collector
because it provides the most flexible and feature-rich architecture for a wide range of use cases
and deployment scenarios.

See [OpenTelemetry Collector](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/collector/) for more information.

Key reasons for using a collector include:

- Compatibility

  Mule runtime sends telemetry signals to one endpoint (the collector) in one (OTEL binary/protobuf)
  format, ensuring vendor neutrality. Then, the collector manages exporting signals to the selected
  backends. This is especially useful for monitoring solutions like Azure, which canât ingest OTEL
  signals directly and require a specific exporter, or Grafana, which implements authentication and
  additional metrics via collector components.

  Exporting the same signal to multiple third-party solutions is only possible through a collector
  because Mule runtime allows one endpoint per signal.

  Most third-party monitoring solutions support or provide their own collector implementations.

- Security

  Applications donât need external Internet access to reach third-party monitoring solutions.

  The collector handles authentication and authorization against third-party monitoring solutions.
  You can use it as a single point for dedicated network policies.

- Performance

  You can optimize collectors for specific goals, scale independently, and distribute load without
  affecting Mule runtime instances. Configuration changes donât affect Mule runtime instances.

- Observability

  Collectors include multiple built-in metrics and health checks.

- Centralize data processing

  A collector can centralize data enrichment and transformation for Mule runtime telemetry and
  additional non-Mule services.

### Direct Telemetry Stream Configuration

Mule runtime includes predefined configuration templates for exporting traces and logs.

#### Hybrid Standalone Deployment

Configure hybrid instances by using
[Runtime Manager hybrid server properties](../../hybrid-standalone/managing-servers#configure-server-properties)
instead of Java system properties.

Additionally, you can configure a limited set of options at the application level by using
[application deployment properties](https://docs.mulesoft.com/hybrid-standalone/deploying-apps-hybrid-standalone#deployment-configuration).
This configuration is limited to a subset of the available settings.

Service Identification

- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.resource.service.name`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.resource.service.namespace`

Sampling

- `mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.sampler.type`
- `mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.sampler.arg`

mTLS

- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.certificatesStrategy`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.certFileLocation`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.keyFileLocation`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.caFileLocation`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.certBase64`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.keyBase64`
- `mule.openTelemetry.exporter.tls.caBase64`

#### CloudHub, CloudHub 2.0 and Anypoint Runtime Fabric Deployments

Configure these deployments by using
[Runtime Manager application properties](configuring-properties#setting-properties-values-in-runtime-manager).

### Direct Telemetry Stream for Traces Configuration

Tracing export setup properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.enabled</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Set to <code>true</code> to enable the Direct Telemetry Stream tracing exporter.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.endpoint</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>http://localhost:4317</code> for GRPC, <code>http://localhost:4318/v1/traces</code> for HTTP</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint where the traces are exported to.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.type</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>GRPC</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Transport type. Available values: <code>GRPC</code>, <code>HTTP</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.headers</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty list</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Comma-separated list of headers included in every export call made to the export endpoint. Example: <code>headerName1=headerValue1,headerName2=headerValue2</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

Tracing export tuning properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.level</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MONITORING</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Mule runtime tracing level. Available values: <code>OVERVIEW</code>, <code>MONITORING</code>, <code>DEBUG</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.sampler</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>parentbased_traceidratio</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Head sampling strategy used. Available values: <code>always_on</code>, <code>always_off</code>, <code>traceidratio</code>, <code>parentbased_always_off</code>, <code>parentbased_traceidratio</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.sampler.arg</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Argument for head sampling strategy. For example, sampling percentage for the <code>traceidratio</code> strategy.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.timeout</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>10 Seconds</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout for every export call made to the export endpoint.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.compression</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>none</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Compression algorithm used to compress the tracing data to be sent on every export call made to the export endpoint. Available values: <code>none</code>, <code>gzip</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.backoff.maxAttempts</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>5</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum retry attempts to export a batch of spans when the export endpoint responds with a retryable error.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.backoff.initial</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1 second</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Wait time before the first retry attempt.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.backoff.maximum</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>5 seconds</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum wait time before a retry attempt.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.backoff.multiplier</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.5</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Multiplier applied to the last wait time before a new retry attempt.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.batch.scheduledDelay</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>5000 milliseconds</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum interval between two consecutive exports.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.batch.queueSize</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2048 spans</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Export queue size. When full, non-fitting spans are handled according to the configured backpressure strategy. A <code>WARNING</code> log is triggered. Example: <code>Export queue overflow: 1 spans have been dropped. Total spans dropped since the export started: 2</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.batch.maxSize</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>512 spans</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum size of batches exported per span.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.batch.backPressure.strategy</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>DROP</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Strategy for handling full export queues. Available values: <code>BLOCK</code> or <code>DROP</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

Minimal configuration example that exports 50% of the traces to an HTTP OpenTelemetry collector
endpoint:

mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.enabled=true mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.type=HTTP
mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.endpoint=https://your-collector.com/api/v1/traces
mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.sampler.arg=0.5

> [!NOTE] The `mule.openTelemetry.tracer.exporter.sampler.arg` represents the percentage of sampling
> where 0.1 is 10% and 1 is 100%.

### Direct Telemetry Stream for Logs Configuration

Logging export setup properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.enabled</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Set to <code>true</code> to enable the Direct Telemetry Stream logging exporter.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.endpoint</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>http://localhost:4317</code> for GRPC, <code>http://localhost:4318/v1/logs</code> for HTTP</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint for exporting logs.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.type</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>GRPC</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Transport type. Logging export tuning properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.level</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>INFO</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Minimum severity level to export. Mule doesnât send log records below this level to the export endpoint. Available values: <code>TRACE</code>, <code>DEBUG</code>, <code>INFO</code>, <code>WARN</code>, <code>ERROR</code>, and <code>FATAL</code>. Extended OpenTelemetry severity levels, such as <code>INFO2</code> or <code>WARN3</code>, are accepted and normalized to their base level before Mule applies them because Log4j doesnât support extended levels.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.timeout</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>10 Seconds</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout for every export call made to the export endpoint.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.compression</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>none</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Compression algorithm used to compress the logging data to be sent on every export call made to the export endpoint. When full, non-fitting log records are handled according to the configured backpressure strategy. A <code>WARNING</code> log is triggered. Example: <code>Export queue overflow: 1 log records have been dropped. Total dropped log records since the export started: 2</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.batch.maxSize</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>512 log records</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum size of batches exported per log record.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.batch.backPressure.strategy</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>BLOCK</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Strategy for handling full export queues. Available values: <code>BLOCK</code> or <code>DROP</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] Use `mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.level` only as a Direct Telemetry Stream export
> filter. Configure actual logging levels in the Log4j configuration.
>
> For example, to export `DEBUG` logs for a specific Java package, set the package level in your
> Log4j configuration file:
>
> ```xml
> <Loggers>
>     <AsyncLogger name="com.example.myapp" level="DEBUG"/>
>     <AsyncRoot level="INFO"/>
> </Loggers>
> ```
>
> With `mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.level=DEBUG`, Mule exports `DEBUG` logs produced by
> `com.example.myapp`. With `mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.level=INFO` (default), Log4j still
> generates and processes those `DEBUG` logs, but Mule doesnât export them to the Direct Telemetry
> Stream endpoint.

Minimal configuration example that sends logs to an HTTP OpenTelemetry collector endpoint:

mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.enabled=true mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.type=HTTP
mule.openTelemetry.logging.exporter.endpoint=https://your-collector.com/api/v1/logs
mule.put.trace.id.and.span.id.in.mdc=true

> [!NOTE] Setting the `mule.put.trace.id.and.span.id.in.mdc` property to `true` wonât be necessary
> in future releases because Mule runtime will automatically enable it.
